> A **bootable USB drive** is a USB flash drive that contains a complete, bootable operating system installer. The computer's firmware (BIOS/UEFI) can detect this USB at startup and launch the OS installation program directly from it, without needing the host OS to be running.

**Two Tools to Create Bootable USB:**
1. **Rufus** — A lightweight, open-source utility for Windows that creates bootable USB drives from ISO images. Supports both MBR and GPT partition schemes.
2. **balenaEtcher** — A cross-platform flashing tool that supports Windows, macOS, and Linux. Known for its simple three-step interface: select image, select drive, flash.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for tools)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to install **Ubuntu Linux 22.04 LTS** on a desktop computer. Include details on disk partitioning and bootloader configuration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The installation of Ubuntu Linux 22.04 LTS proceeds through the following stages:

**Step 1: Preparation Phase**
- Download the official ISO from [ubuntu.com/download](https://ubuntu.com/download).
- Verify the SHA-256 checksum of the ISO.
- Create a bootable USB using **Rufus** (Windows) or **balenaEtcher** (Linux/macOS).
- Backup all critical data from the target machine.

**Step 2: BIOS/UEFI Configuration**
- Restart and press the BIOS key (**F2/F10/DEL**).
- Set **USB as the first boot device**.
- For UEFI systems, ensure **UEFI mode** is enabled.
- Save and exit.

**Step 3: Booting the Installer**
- The system boots from USB, displaying the **GRUB menu**.
- Select **"Install Ubuntu"**.
- Choose the **language**, **keyboard layout**, and connect to **Wi-Fi**.

**Step 4: Disk Partitioning**
Choose **"Something else"** for manual partitioning and create:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Partition 1 (EFI):} & \quad 512 \text{ MB}, \text{ FAT32, EFI System Partition} \\
\text{Partition 2 (/):} & \quad 25 \text{ GB minimum}, \text{ ext4, Mount point: /} \\
\text{Partition 3 (swap):} & \quad 2 \times \text{RAM size}, \text{ Linux-swap} \\
\text{Partition 4 (/home):} & \quad \text{Remaining space}, \text{ ext4, Mount point: /home}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: User Account Setup**
- Enter real name, computer name, username, and password.
- Enable **"Require password to login"**.

**Step 6: Installation and Bootloader**
- The installer copies files, installs the **GRUB bootloader** to the EFI partition.
- The system reboots automatically.

**Step 7: Post-Installation**
- Remove the USB when prompted.
- Login with the created credentials.
- Run `sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade` to update the system.

*(7 marks: 1 for preparation, 2 for partitioning with details, 2 for installation steps, 2 for bootloader and post-install)*

---

**(b)** Describe the procedure to create a **dual-boot system** with **Windows 11 and Ubuntu Linux** on the same machine. Justify why one OS should be installed before the other. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Dual-Boot Setup Procedure:**

**Step 1: Install Windows First**
- Install Windows 11 on the entire disk (or a primary partition).
- Reserve unallocated space for Linux.

**Step 2: Shrink Windows Partition**
- In Windows **Disk Management**, right-click the Windows partition → **Shrink Volume**.
- Allocate space for Linux (e.g., **100 GB**).

**Step 3: Boot from Ubuntu USB**
- Insert the Ubuntu bootable USB.
- Boot into the Ubuntu installer.

**Step 4: Install Ubuntu on Free Space**
- Choose **"Install alongside Windows"** (automatic option), OR
- Choose **"Something else"** for manual partitioning on the unallocated space.

**Step 5: GRUB Auto-Detection**
- During installation, GRUB automatically detects the existing Windows installation.
- GRUB is installed as the primary bootloader in the **EFI System Partition**.

**Step 6: Reboot and Choose**
- On reboot, the **GRUB menu** displays both:
  - `Ubuntu`
  - `Windows Boot Manager (on /dev/sda1)`
- Use arrow keys to select the desired OS.

**Justification for Order (Why Windows First?):**

> The **Windows Boot Manager** is *non-cooperative* and tends to overwrite any existing bootloader when installed second, thereby destroying the Linux GRUB. Conversely, **GRUB** is *cooperative* and can chainload the Windows Boot Manager, allowing both OSes to coexist. Therefore, the safe installation order is **Windows → Linux**.

*(7 marks: 2 for steps, 2 for GRUB behavior, 1 for partitioning, 2 for justification with order rule)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to install **Windows 11** on a desktop computer. Include the disk partitioning scheme and post-installation steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Download and Create Bootable Media**
- Use the official **Microsoft Media Creation Tool**.
- Select **"Create installation media for another PC"**.
- Choose **USB flash drive** (minimum **8 GB**).
- The tool downloads the ISO and writes it to the USB.

**Step 2: Configure UEFI/BIOS**
- Boot into BIOS by pressing **F2 / DEL** during startup.
- In the **Boot** tab:
  - Disable **Secure Boot** (if necessary).
  - Set **USB as the first boot priority**.
  - Enable **UEFI mode**.
- Save and exit.

**Step 3: Begin Windows Installation**
- The system boots from USB into **Windows Setup**.
- Choose language, time format, and keyboard.
- Click **"Install Now"**.
- Enter the **25-character product key** (or skip).
- Select **Windows 11** edition.
- Accept the **license terms**.

**Step 4: Custom Disk Partitioning**
- Choose **"Custom: Install Windows only (advanced)"**.
- The installer shows available disks and partitions.
- For a clean install on unallocated space:
  - Click **New** → enter size in MB → **Apply**.
  - Windows automatically creates:
    - **Partition 1**: System (Recovery) — ~500 MB
    - **Partition 2**: MSR (Microsoft Reserved) — ~16 MB
    - **Partition 3**: Windows (C:) — Remaining space, NTFS
- Select **Partition 3** and click **Next**.

**Step 5: File Copy and Installation**
- Windows copies installation files.
- Multiple automatic restarts occur.
- Remove the USB when prompted (after the first restart).

**Step 6: Out-of-Box Experience (OOBE)**
- Select **region** and **keyboard layout**.
- Connect to **Wi-Fi**.
- Sign in with a **Microsoft Account** (or create a local account).
- Configure **privacy settings** (telemetry, location, diagnostics).

**Step 7: Post-Installation**
- Verify activation: **Settings → System → Activation**.
- Run **Windows Update** to install latest patches.
- Install **device drivers** (GPU, audio, network).
- Install essential software (browser, office suite).

*(7 marks: 1 for media creation, 1 for BIOS, 2 for partitioning, 1 for installation, 2 for OOBE and post-install)*

---

**(b)** Compare **Legacy BIOS (MBR)** and **UEFI (GPT)** boot modes. Why is UEFI preferred in modern systems? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Legacy BIOS with MBR | UEFI with GPT |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Basic Input/Output System | Unified Extensible Firmware Interface |
| Partition Table | MBR (Master Boot Record) | GPT (GUID Partition Table) |
| Max Partitions | 4 primary | 128 primary |
| Max Disk Size | 2 TB | 9.4 ZB |
| Boot Speed | Slower (POST + MBR scan) | Faster (parallel initialization) |
| Security | None | Secure Boot support |
| User Interface | Text-based | Graphical mouse-driven |
| Disk Signature | 32-bit | 64-bit CRC32 |
| Redundancy | None | Backup partition table |
| Boot Mode | 16-bit real mode | 32/64-bit protected mode |

**Why UEFI is Preferred:**

1. **Larger Disk Support**: UEFI + GPT supports disks larger than 2 TB, essential for modern high-capacity drives.

2. **Faster Boot Times**: UEFI initializes hardware in parallel, while BIOS does so sequentially. This results in noticeably faster startup.

3. **Secure Boot**: UEFI's **Secure Boot** feature verifies the digital signature of bootloaders and kernels, preventing rootkit and bootkit malware from executing.

4. **More Partitions**: GPT allows up to **128 primary partitions**, eliminating the need for extended partitions and logical drives.

5. **Graphical Interface**: UEFI provides a **mouse-driven, graphical setup interface**, making configuration more intuitive.

6. **Modular Design**: UEFI supports **network boot (PXE)**, **shell environment**, and is firmware-independent.

7. **Windows 11 Requirement**: Microsoft **mandates UEFI + Secure Boot + TPM 2.0** for Windows 11 installation, making UEFI a de facto standard.

*(7 marks: 4 for table, 3 for reasons with examples)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MBR and GPT**: Many students incorrectly state that MBR supports more than 4 partitions. The correct answer is **MBR = 4 primary partitions max**; **GPT = 128 primary partitions**.
> 2. **Wrong Installation Order in Dual-Boot**: Always state **Windows first, then Linux**. Reversing this loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting Swap Partition**: Linux installations require a swap partition. Students who skip this lose marks in partitioning questions.
> 4. **Not Mentioning Secure Boot**: In Windows 11 installation questions, always mention the **Secure Boot** and **TPM 2.0** requirements.
> 5. **Skipping Verification Step**: The post-installation verification (`uname -a`, `lsblk`, `df -h` for Linux) is often expected by KTU examiners.
> 6. **Wrong Bootloader Names**: Confusing GRUB with GRUB2, or calling Windows Boot Manager "boot.ini" (legacy Windows XP terminology).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **OS Installation** = deploying kernel + bootloader + files to storage.
- ✅ **Bootable USB** is created using **Rufus** or **balenaEtcher**.
- ✅ **BIOS key** is typically **F2, F10, F12, DEL, or ESC** (varies by manufacturer).
- ✅ **UEFI** uses **GPT**; **Legacy BIOS** uses **MBR**.
- ✅ **GPT** supports up to **128 partitions** and disks up to **9.4 ZB**.
- ✅ **MBR** supports max **4 primary partitions** and **2 TB** disks.
- ✅ **Windows** file system: **NTFS**; **Linux** file system: **ext4**.
- ✅ **EFI System Partition**: **512 MB, FAT32** (used by UEFI bootloaders).
- ✅ **Linux swap partition** size: **2 × RAM** (rule of thumb).
- ✅ **Linux root partition (/)** minimum size: **25 GB**.
- ✅ **GRUB** is the default Linux bootloader and can chainload Windows.
- ✅ **Windows Boot Manager** overwrites GRUB if installed second.
- ✅ **Installation Order for Dual-Boot**: **Windows → Linux** (always).
- ✅ **Windows 11 requires**: UEFI + Secure Boot + TPM 2.0.
- ✅ **Linux verification commands**: `uname -a`, `lsblk`, `df -h`, `sudo apt update`.
- ✅ **Lab Record Evidence**: Screenshots of boot menu, partitioning, desktop, and About page are mandatory for full marks.
- ✅ **Post-install Linux updates**: `sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y`.
- ✅ **Common file system for USB drives**: **FAT32** (universal) or **exFAT** (large files).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic**: **"W-L-G-M-N-S"** for dual-boot order: **W**indows → **L**inux → **G**RUB → **M**enu → **N**TFS/ext4 → **S**ecure boot.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarizing Basic Unix/Linux Commands - ls, mkdir, grep, rmdir, chmod, useradd, passwd, history, dmesg, cpuinfo, uname, time, write, fdisk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXESL208 - IT WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarizing Basic Unix/Linux Commands - ls, mkdir, grep, rmdir, chmod, useradd, passwd, history, dmesg, cpuinfo, uname, time, write, fdisk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **IT Workshop (GXESL208)** lab curriculum under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, a *Unix/Linux command* is a **discrete, executable instruction issued to the operating system shell** that interacts with the **kernel** to perform file system operations, process management, user administration, hardware interrogation, and inter-process communication.

The shell acts as a **command-line interpreter** that parses input strings (commands + options + arguments) and translates them into **system calls** such as `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `fork()`, `exec()`, and `stat()`. Every command listed in this experiment—`ls`, `mkdir`, `grep`, `rmdir`, `chmod`, `useradd`, `passwd`, `history`, `dmesg`, `cpuinfo` (technically the file `/proc/cpuinfo`), `uname`, `time`, `write`, and `fdisk`—resides either as a **compiled binary** in standard paths like `/bin`, `/usr/bin`, or `/sbin`, or as a **shell builtin** (e.g., `history` in Bash).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Course Outcome Mapping**
> This experiment directly satisfies **CO2**: *"Use basic Unix/Linux commands for file management, user administration, and system resource monitoring."* It also touches **CO3** (problem-solving using shell tools) and is mapped to **Bloom's Level L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Workspace" Model

Imagine the Linux operating system as a **large, well-organized office building**:

- The **file system** is the building's *filing cabinet system* (folders, sub-folders, files).
- The **shell** is the *receptionist* who listens to your verbal instructions and dispatches the right office worker.
- Each **command** is a *specialist worker*—`ls` is the inventory clerk, `mkdir` is the carpenter who builds new cabinets, `chmod` is the security officer who decides who can enter which room, `useradd` is the HR manager, and `fdisk` is the facility engineer who partitions the storage warehouse.
- The **kernel** is the *building manager* sitting in the basement, who actually turns lights on and locks doors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Commands in Unix/Linux are **case-sensitive**. `ls` works; `LS` does not. Paths and filenames are also case-sensitive. This is a critical distinction from Windows/DOS.

## 1.3 The Command Syntax Convention (Universal Pattern)

Every command in this experiment follows the **POSIX-standardized syntax**:

```
command [options] [arguments]
```

Where:
- **`command`** — the program name (e.g., `ls`, `chmod`).
- **`options`** — flags (usually prefixed with `-` for short form or `--` for long form) that modify behavior (e.g., `ls -l`).
- **`arguments`** — targets the command operates upon (e.g., filenames, usernames, devices).

This three-part structure is the **universal grammar** of all Unix-like systems, inherited from the original 1970s Bell Labs design philosophy that KTU examiners frequently test.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Commands (Logical Grouping for the Lab Record)

The 14 commands covered in this experiment fall into **seven functional categories**. Understanding this taxonomy is essential for the **ESE viva voce** and **record-writing** components.

### Category 1: File & Directory Operations
| Command | Full Form | Primary Purpose |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| `ls` | **L**i**s**t | Enumerate directory contents |
| `mkdir` | **M**a**k**e **dir**ectory | Create new directories |
| `rmdir` | **R**e**m**ove **dir**ectory | Delete *empty* directories |

### Category 2: Text Searching
| Command | Full Form | Primary Purpose |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| `grep` | **G**lobal **R**egular **E**xpression **P**rint | Pattern-match text within files |

### Category 3: File Permissions
| Command | Full Form | Primary Purpose |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| `chmod` | **Ch**ange **mod**e | Modify file/directory permission bits |

### Category 4: User Management
| Command | Full Form | Primary Purpose |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| `useradd` | **User add** | Create a new user account |
| `passwd` | **Pass**wor**d** | Set/change user password |

### Category 5: System Information
| Command | Full Form | Primary Purpose |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| `dmesg` | **D**isplay **mes**sa**g**e (driver message) | Print kernel ring buffer |
| `cpuinfo` | `/proc/cpuinfo` | CPU hardware details |
| `uname` | **U**nix **name** | Print system info |
| `time` | **Time** | Measure command execution time |

### Category 6: Inter-User Communication
| Command | Full Form | Primary Purpose |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| `write` | **Write** | Send a message to another logged-in user |

### Category 7: Disk & Session Utilities
| Command | Full Form | Primary Purpose |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| `fdisk` | **F**ixed **disk** | Manipulate disk partition table |
| `history` | **History** | Display previously typed commands |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — The chmod Octal Notation

The most mathematically rigorous portion of this experiment is the **octal permission calculation** for `chmod`. Every digit is the **sum of three independent bits** as shown in the table below. This is the **single most-tested concept** in the ESE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** The permission bits form a **base-8 (octal) numeral system** where each digit is the sum of the bit weights $4$, $2$, $1$. This is **not** arbitrary—it maps directly to the file mode bits stored in the `inode` structure of the ext4/Btrfs file system.

| Permission Bit | Symbol | Octal Value | Binary |
|----------------|--------|-------------|--------|
| Read | `r` | **4** | $2^{2}$ |
| Write | `w` | **2** | $2^{1}$ |
| Execute | `x` | **1** | $2^{0}$ |
| No permission | `-` | **0** | $0$ |

A complete `chmod` mode is a **3-digit octal number** $d_1 d_2 d_3$ where:

- $d_1$ = permission for the **owner (user / u)**
- $d_2$ = permission for the **group (g)**
- $d_3$ = permission for **others (o)**

Each digit satisfies:

$$d_i = (r \times 4) + (w \times 2) + (x \times 1), \quad d_i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The same octal encoding is used in the `stat()` system call's `st_mode` field, in C header `<sys/stat.h>` macros (`S_IRUSR`, `S_IWUSR`, etc.), and in POSIX ACLs. Understanding this encoding is foundational for **system programming**, **embedded Linux**, and **cybersecurity** (penetration testing of file permissions).

## 2.3 Why chmod Has Two Syntaxes

The `chmod` utility supports two equivalent syntaxes because they solve different cognitive tasks:

- **Symbolic mode** (`chmod u+x file`) — *human-readable*, best for incremental permission changes.
- **Octal/Numeric mode** (`chmod 755 file`) — *compact*, best for scripts and setting all permissions at once.

The symbolic syntax uses **three operator classes** $\{$ `u`, `g`, `o`, `a` (all) $\}$, **three operators** $\{$ `+` (add), `-` (remove), `=` (set exactly) $\}$, and **three permission letters** $\{$ `r`, `w`, `x` $\}$.

## 2.4 The /proc Virtual File System — Foundation for `dmesg` and `cpuinfo`

Linux exposes kernel and hardware data through a **virtual, in-memory file system** mounted at `/proc`. Unlike disk-backed file systems, every read of a `/proc` file dynamically invokes a kernel function that formats the requested data. This is why:

- `/proc/cpuinfo` → returns CPU information without consuming disk space.
- `dmesg` → reads from the kernel's **ring buffer** in memory (size typically **16384 bytes** / 16 KB on modern kernels, though configurable via `CONFIG_LOG_BUF_SHIFT`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `cpuinfo` is **not** a command—it is a file. The "command" to read it is `cat /proc/cpuinfo` or the wrapper `lscpu`. Many students incorrectly write `cpuinfo` as a command in the lab record and lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — chmod Octal Permissions

### Worked Example A: `chmod 755 script.sh`

We must translate the octal triplet $755$ into a permission string.

**Step 1 — Decompose each digit by class position:**

The triplet $d_1 d_2 d_3 = 7 \, 5 \, 5$ corresponds to:
- $d_1 = 7$ → owner (user)
- $d_2 = 5$ → group
- $d_3 = 5$ → others

**Step 2 — Apply the bit-weight sum to the owner ($d_1 = 7$):**

$$7 = 4 + 2 + 1 \implies r = 1, \quad w = 1, \quad x = 1 \implies \mathbf{rwx}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to the group ($d_2 = 5$):**

$$5 = 4 + 0 + 1 \implies r = 1, \quad w = 0, \quad x = 1 \implies \mathbf{r\text{-}x}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to others ($d_3 = 5$):**

$$5 = 4 + 0 + 1 \implies r = 1, \quad w = 0, \quad x = 1 \implies \mathbf{r\text{-}x}$$

**Step 5 — Concatenate the three triplets to form the symbolic string:**

$$\text{Final permission string} = \mathbf{rwxr\text{-}xr\text{-}x}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation in `ls -l` output:**

```
-rwxr-xr-x 1 user user 2048 Nov 15 10:30 script.sh
 ^^^ ^^^^^ ^^^^^
  u    g    o
```

### Worked Example B: `chmod 644 report.txt`

**Step 1 — Decompose $644$:**
- Owner → $6$, Group → $4$, Others → $4$.

**Step 2 — Owner ($d_1 = 6$):**

$$6 = 4 + 2 + 0 \implies \mathbf{rw\text{-}}$$

**Step 3 — Group ($d_2 = 4$):**

$$4 = 4 + 0 + 0 \implies \mathbf{r\text{--}}$$

**Step 4 — Others ($d_3 = 4$):**

$$4 = 4 + 0 + 0 \implies \mathbf{r\text{--}}$$

**Step 5 — Final permission string:**

$$\text{Final permission string} = \mathbf{rw\text{-}r\text{--}r\text{--}}$$

This is the **default permission for newly created regular files** under the standard umask of $022$ (a frequently tested fact).

### Symbolic Equivalence Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Octal | Binary (r,w,x) | Symbolic | Common Use Case |
|------:|:--------------:|:--------:|:----------------|
| $0$ | $000$ | $\text{---}$ | No access (paranoid) |
| $1$ | $001$ | $\text{--x}$ | Execute only |
| $2$ | $010$ | $\text{-w-}$ | Write only (rare) |
| $3$ | $011$ | $\text{-wx}$ | Write + execute |
| $4$ | $100$ | $r\text{--}$ | Read only |
| $5$ | $101$ | $r\text{-}x$ | Read + execute (typical script) |
| $6$ | $110$ | $rw\text{-}$ | Read + write (typical data file) |
| $7$ | $111$ | $rwx$ | Full access (typical program/dir) |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Walkthrough — `grep` Pattern Matching

`grep` scans files line-by-line and outputs lines matching a **regular expression**. The algorithmic flow is:

1. Open the target file(s).
2. Read line $L_i$ sequentially.
3. Apply the **regex engine** (POSIX BRE/ERE) to test if the pattern matches.
4. If match → write $L_i$ to standard output; else → skip.
5. Repeat until EOF.

**Example session (analytical walkthrough):**

```bash
$ cat > fruits.txt << EOF
apple
banana
apricot
cherry
grape
EOF

$ grep "^a" fruits.txt
apple
apricot
```

**Line-by-line trace:**

- $L_1 =$ `"apple"` → regex `^a` matches (starts with `a`) → **print**.
- $L_2 =$ `"banana"` → does not start with `a` → skip.
- $L_3 =$ `"apricot"` → starts with `a` → **print**.
- $L_4 =$ `"cherry"` → skip.
- $L_5 =$ `"grape"` → starts with `g` → skip.

**Result:** 2 lines printed.

**Common `grep` options (KTU-favorite):**

| Option | Meaning | Example |
|:------:|---------|---------|
| `-i` | Case-insensitive | `grep -i "ERROR" log.txt` |
| `-n` | Show line numbers | `grep -n "main" *.c` |
| `-v` | Invert match | `grep -v "^#" config.ini` |
| `-r` | Recursive into subdirs | `grep -r "TODO" src/` |
| `-c` | Count matches only | `grep -c "fail" log.txt` |
| `-l` | List filenames only | `grep -rl "secret" /home` |
| `-E` | Extended regex (ERE) | `grep -E "(error|warn)" log` |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — All 14 Commands at a Glance

The matrix below is the **single most important revision table** for this experiment. Memorize the *Purpose* and at least *one* option for each.

| # | Command | Syntax Template | Core Purpose | Most-Used Option |
|:-:|---------|-----------------|--------------|:----------------:|
| 1 | `ls` | `ls [opts] [path]` | List directory entries | `-l$ (long format) |
| 2 | `mkdir` | `mkdir [opts] dir` | Create new directory | $-p$ (create parents) |
| 3 | `grep` | `grep [opts] pat file` | Search text by pattern | $-i$ (case-insensitive) |
| 4 | `rmdir` | `rmdir [opts] dir` | Remove *empty* directory | $-p$ (remove parents) |
| 5 | `chmod` | `chmod [opts] mode file` | Change file permissions | $-R$ (recursive) |
| 6 | `useradd` | `useradd [opts] user` | Create new user account | $-m$ (create home) |
| 7 | `passwd` | `passwd [user]` | Set/change password | (no flag — interactive) |

| 9 | `dmesg` | `dmesg [opts]` | Print kernel messages | $\vert$ `grep` pipe |

| 12 | `time` | `time command` | Time a command's run | (built-in) |

## 3.4 Detailed Option Breakdowns (Lab Record Essentials)

### `ls` — five essential flags
- `ls` → short list (names only).
- `ls -l` → long format: shows permissions, links, owner, group, size, mtime, name.
- `ls -a` → include **hidden files** (those starting with `.`).
- `ls -h` → **human-readable** sizes (KB, MB, GB) — combine with `-l` as `-lh`.
- `ls -R` → **recursive** listing of sub-directories.

### `mkdir` and `rmdir` — the create/delete pair
- `mkdir project` → creates a *single* directory.
- `mkdir -p src/utils/helpers` → creates the entire nested path; does not fail if intermediate dirs already exist.
- `rmdir old` → removes an *empty* directory; fails non-fatally if the directory contains files.
- `rmdir -p a/b/c` → removes `c`, then `b`, then `a`, provided each is empty.

### `chmod` — symbolic examples
- `chmod u+x script.sh` → add execute for the user.
- `chmod g-w file.txt` → remove write from the group.
- `chmod o=r public.txt` → set others' permission to read-only (overwrites prior).
- `chmod a+x runme` → add execute for *all* classes (user, group, other).
- `chmod -R 755 /srv/www` → recursively set the entire web tree.

### `useradd` and `passwd` — the user lifecycle
- `useradd -m -s /bin/bash john` → create user `john` with home directory and Bash shell.
- `useradd -g developers -G sudo,git alice` → primary group + supplementary groups.
- `passwd john` → (as root) set password for `john`.
- `passwd` → (as `john`) change *own* password.

### `history` — Bash builtin
- `history` → list all retained commands (default buffer = 1000 in Bash).
- `history 20` → last 20 commands.
- `!!` → re-execute the previous command.
- `!42` → re-execute command #42 from history.
- `Ctrl-R` → interactive **reverse search** through history (KTU favorite trick question).

### `dmesg` and `uname` — kernel diagnostics
- `dmesg | tail -20` → last 20 kernel messages (boot, hardware detection, errors).
- `dmesg | grep -i "usb"` → only USB-related kernel events.
- `uname -a` → all info: kernel name, hostname, kernel version, architecture.
- `uname -r` → kernel **release** only (e.g., `5.15.0-91-generic`).
- `uname -m` → machine hardware (e.g., `x86_64`).

### `cpuinfo` — the /proc file
- `cat /proc/cpuinfo` → dump every CPU field for every core.
- `lscpu` → concise, formatted summary (preferred for lab records).
- `nproc` → count of available processing units.

### `time` — performance timing
- `time ls -R /` → outputs three lines: **real** (wall-clock), **user** (CPU in user mode), **sys** (CPU in kernel mode).
- Note: There are *two* `time` commands — a Bash builtin and `/usr/bin/time`. They differ in formatting; the builtin is default.

### `write` — terminal messaging
- `write alice` → opens a real-time message session to logged-in user `alice`.
- `write alice pts/2` → specify the terminal.
- End the message with **Ctrl-D** or EOF.
- The recipient must have `mesg y` enabled (default).

### `fdisk` — disk partitioning
- `sudo fdisk -l` → **list** all detected partitions (no edits).
- `sudo fdisk /dev/sda` → interactive mode for `/dev/sda`.
- Inside fdisk: `m` (help), `p` (print), `n` (new), `d` (delete), `w` (write), `q` (quit).
- **WARNING:** Writing a new partition table **destroys all data** on the disk.
